deck_meta.title: What 81,000 People Told Us
deck_meta.subtitle: Productivity, exposure, and the felt economics of AI

slides.title:
What 81,000 People
Told Us About
the Economics of AI
slides.subtitle: Productivity, exposure, and the felt economics of AI — a survey of 81,000 Claude users
slides.byline: Maxim Massenkoff & Saffron Huang  ·  Anthropic Research  ·  2026

slides.title:
From what Claude does —
to how its users feel.
slides.lede: Prior Anthropic research mapped Claude usage onto the U.S. occupation system. This survey is the first qualitative bridge — connecting observed exposure with what workers actually feel about the displacement risk.

from_block.label: Before this study
points: Economic Index measured what tasks Claude performs
Observed exposure quantified % of role's tasks AI touches
But: no link to worker sentiment or felt threat

to_block.label: What this study adds
points: 81,000 self-selected Claude.ai users surveyed in-product
Open-ended interview format — not multiple choice
LLM classifiers extract occupation, career stage, sentiment
Direct link from exposure data to perceived job threat

slides.section_title: Three headline patterns the survey makes visible.
preview: Productivity gains are real and substantial
Speedup and anxiety form a U-shape

slides.title: The headline numbers.
slides.subtitle: Productivity gains are real — anxiety scales with exposure.

stats.label: Mean productivity rating
stats.note: Self-reported on 1–7 scale. 5 corresponds to “substantially more productive.” 3% reported negative or neutral impacts; 42% gave no clear indication.

stats.label: Voice job-displacement concern
stats.note: 20% of respondents directly expressed worry about their own role being lost to AI — with concern concentrated in high-exposure occupations.

stats.label: Top vs bottom exposure quartile
stats.note: People in the top 25% of exposure mentioned the worry three times as often as those in the bottom 25%. +1.3pt threat per 10pt exposure.

stats.label: Scope gains / Speed gains
stats.note: 48% of users who mentioned productivity cited scope gains (new abilities). 40% cited speed gains (faster execution of existing work).

slides.footer_note: Source: §Who benefits from AI?  ·  §Who worries about displacement?  ·  Figure 1

preview: Worker intuitions track the usage data — anxiety is rational
Career stage shapes who feels safe
Benefits do not equal feeling safe

slides.title:
Worker anxiety is concentrated
where Claude is most active.
slides.subtitle: People's intuitions track the usage data — not paranoia, not panic.
slides.primary_quote: People's intuitions track the usage data: they worry most about AI's effect in the jobs where we observe Claude doing the most work.
slides.secondary_quote: Like anyone who has a white collar job these days, I'm 100% concerned, pretty much 24/7 concerned about losing my job eventually to A.I.
slides.implication: Workers can feel the diffusion of AI in their own occupations long before formal labor statistics show displacement. The fear is rational — and concentrated exactly where AI use is highest.

slides.section_title: How AI restructures who wins — and how.
preview: Scope before speed — access, not just efficiency
Productivity is bimodal across the wage distribution
Speedup and anxiety form a U-shape

slides.title: Scope and speed — not just speed.
slides.subtitle: Most users describe AI unlocking new abilities, not just faster execution of old ones.

points: “I'm a non-tech guy but now I'm a full-stack developer”
Customer-service rep starts e-commerce business
Delivery driver builds e-commerce · landscaper builds music app
Users attempt tasks they could not do before
“15 minutes that used to take 2 hours” — financing task
Faster code reviews, contract checks, paperwork
Quality gains via more thorough checks of code and contracts
Cost gains: replace help that was “over my budget”

slides.callout: AI is not just an efficiency tool — for many users it is an access tool. That distinction shapes who gains and how visibly the gains are felt.

slides.title: Speedup and anxiety form a U-shape.
slides.subtitle: Both extremes — being slowed down and being sped up the most — track with the highest job threat.

points: Some creatives find AI too stifling and rigid to use
Examples: fine artists, certain writers
Still fear AI diffusion into their fields
Highest threat perception among groups in the survey
Largest measured productivity gains in the sample
Above-baseline threat perception
Implicit reasoning: if tasks shrink rapidly, what is left?
Speed gain does not equal job security

slides.callout: The familiar narrative — “AI just makes good workers more productive” — misses half the story. The most empowered users are simultaneously the most worried.

slides.section_title:
Two structural risks hiding behind
the positive top line.
preview: Early-career squeeze — the ladder is eroding from the bottom
Bargaining power decides who keeps the surplus

slides.title: Today's gains hide tomorrow's questions.
slides.subtitle: Positive sentiment is real — but who actually captures the surplus is unresolved.

cards.body: Most workers report benefits flowing to themselves. But a meaningful share say employers or clients are simply extracting more work from the same person.
cards.evidence: 10% of respondents who named a beneficiary said their employer or clients were the ones getting more work from them.
cards.implication: Productivity gain does not equal wage gain. Without bargaining power, the surplus accrues to firms — not workers.

cards.body: The cohort with the most anxiety is also the cohort least confident they personally benefit — a structural mismatch the labor market has not yet priced in.
cards.evidence: Only 60% of early-career workers see themselves benefiting (vs. 80% of senior). Hiring of recent grads is already slowing in the U.S.
cards.implication: The traditional “climb the ladder” path may be eroding from the bottom rungs first.

slides.section_title:
Five concrete moves
for enterprise leaders.
preview: Treat workers' fear as data, not noise
Position AI as an access tool, not a cost tool
Protect early-career pathways now — not later

slides.title: Five moves for enterprise leaders.
slides.subtitle: Workers' fear of AI is rational and predictable — you can act on it.

actions.what: Build your own observed-exposure map: % of each role's tasks already done by AI today.
actions.value: Identify which teams will feel threatened first · prioritize change management

actions.header: Frame AI as scope, not speed
actions.what: Position AI internally as an access tool unlocking new tasks — not as a labor-cost cutter.
actions.value: Aligns with the 48% who frame AI as scope expansion · dampens displacement framing

actions.what: Build AI-augmented apprenticeship programs that explicitly protect early-career development.
actions.value: Counters the early-career squeeze · preserves your future senior pipeline

actions.what: Adopt explicit gain-sharing schemes — bonuses, equity, time-off — tied to AI-driven productivity wins.
actions.value: Closes the employer-capture gap · converts anxiety into engagement

actions.what: Run sentiment listening explicitly with your highest-speedup workers. Do not assume they feel safest.
actions.value: Heads off attrition in highest-leverage staff · turns the U-shape into retention

slides.title:
AI is empowering and unsettling
its users at the same time.
slides.subtitle: The two feelings live in the same people.

pillars.body: The first large-scale qualitative bridge from observed AI usage to user sentiment. Productivity gains are real, broad-based, and skewed bimodally across the wage distribution.
Worker anxiety is not paranoia — it tracks measurable exposure. The most-empowered users are simultaneously the most worried. Career stage shapes who benefits.
Beneficiary capture (who keeps the surplus) and the early-career squeeze (who gets in) are the two structural risks behind the positive top-line numbers.

slides.closing_line: The opportunity is not just to deploy AI — it is to do so in a way that lets the workforce share both the upside and the certainty.

slides.title: Thank you.
slides.subtitle: Curated executive briefs · aidigest.club / reads
